postText:
Bangalore keeps building upward while the ground underneath gets harder to read.

The lakes are shrinking. The traffic doesn't move. Neighbourhoods that were villages fifteen years ago now have seventeen-floor apartments and no footpaths. The city is not failing. It is succeeding so fast that the systems meant to hold it together can't keep up.

I've been looking at ward-level data here. The gap between where infrastructure is planned and where people actually live is striking. Not a planning failure exactly. More like a speed problem. The city outpaces its own maps.

Civic tech can help with that. Real-time GIS layers, open council data, participatory mapping. These tools exist. Some teams are already building with them. But the adoption inside city government is slow. Not because officials don't care. Because trust in new systems takes time, and the procurement pipeline takes longer.

What Bangalore needs is not more dashboards. It needs fewer handoffs between the people who collect data and the people who make decisions with it.

Most Indian cities face some version of this. Bangalore just makes it visible at scale.

If your organisation works at the city level, how short is the chain between your field data and the person who can actually act on it?

#CivicTech
#UrbanPolicy
#GIS
